sentences.text: Yes.
Not only at me, at everybody.
And the people were falling just like flies.
When I turned and I saw, I-- I-- it was the most horrifying thing.
People were lying.
I had to-- when I ran, I had to crawl over many dead.
And only then, I found myself-- I'm still having my sister's hand, Celia, in my hand.
And we were running.
And we came to a wooded area.
And actually, when we went into it, we found out that it was a dangerous place.
Because it was not only wooded, it was a swamp.
And we fell into it.
And it took just a little while that we saw that we were covered with black little leeches.
It was so horrifying.
It was also painful.
But we couldn't do anything about it.
Because suddenly, we heard voices.
And those were people who were running after the survivors who still ran.
They tried to get them.
We were sitting in it quite for a while.
We were there.
And it began to itch and burning sensation.
We couldn't do anything about it, because we heard voices.
And we knew that those weren't Jewish people.
Those were the people who were running after some who still, they thought, had survived.
So we had to stay there.
But we sank even deeper.
Only our heads were out.
And we took some kind of-- it was high grass.
And we covered our heads with it.
After we heard the voices subside and it became very quiet, we tried to crawl out of it, which it was quite a chore.
Because the moment you wanted to move, you were sinking even deeper.
So we decided, since I was smaller and younger, and I didn't weigh that much, that I would try to crawl first.
I did crawl out.
And I found a stem.
And I stood on that stem.
And I gave a hand to my sister, Celia.
And somehow, I-- I took her out.
And we looked absolutely horrible.
We had to clean ourselves of the black little leeches.
And we start walking.
We got out of the swamp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we start walking towards dry land.
And then, we saw.
We came to the edge of the woods, and we saw there a farm.
For-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when we start walking towards the farm, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some kind of miracle.
We saw my sister running out of that house and screaming to us, my sister Chaya.
She was there with my aunt screaming to us and showing us with her arm that we should hide.
We should run.
We should go away.
At that time, we didn't know what it was.
But police was in this house.
And by her running out, they shot her on the spot and also my aunt.
We were hiding.
We laid down in the woods.
We hid there, and we saw it.
But she still had the-- when she saw us, apparently, I don't know how she managed to show us that we shouldn't go closer there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We laid there in the woods until it got really dark.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, we discuss what we should do.
Celia remembered that not very far from there, it was a farm.
The name was Lapuszyno.
And there lived a family.
The name was Bielewicz.
We were very friendly with the family.
One of their sons used to come to our home.
He went to school with Celia.
And he used to come to our home.
And I knew him, and Celia knew him very well.
We decided maybe we could get some kind of refuge there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was night.
We really came there.
They let us into the house, and Piotr decided that he's going to hide us.
He told the family that he's just keeping us for a day, but this wasn't true.
He made-- in the hay in the barn, he dug a hole.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he let us stay there in this whole.
And all day, we were covered with the hay.
In the night, he would come bring us some food.
And this was a time then we could go out to the bathroom.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it went on like that for several weeks.
At this point, I didn't know a thing.
I only knew when I saw my sister Chaya being killed.
The rest of my family, at that time, I didn't know anything.
If any survived, or if everybody was killed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We stayed in that haystack for a while.
And then he became more bold.

And he decided that my sister Celia, since she looked so beautiful, she was blond, she spoke perfect Polish-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that he took her into the house and told everybody of the other surrounding-- the people, that this came a cousin to stay with them for the summer.
She was in the house.
I was left in that pit of the hay.
The mice had a ball with me.
I couldn't move.
I couldn't say anything, because people were working in this barn.
And the mice was-- were nibbling.
And I-- I was so frightened.
I was so-- but I couldn't utter anything.
I couldn't say anything until, of course, the night would come and the people would leave the barn.
And then I would come out of there.
You know, until today, mice, to me, is the most horrifying feeling that I can even remember.
I was sometimes bitten and eaten, and I couldn't say a thing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then suddenly, Piotr came and told us both that people came from the nearby town-- which it was Glebokie-- that they are collecting.
Whoever is left and alive, are collecting to come to the ghetto.
And they promise that they will give them refuge.
They need people to work.
And that things will be OK again.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Celia and myself asked Piotr to go himself to Glebokie and find out.
Because we thought, by any miracle, that maybe somebody survived of our family.
He went there.
He came in-- he went into the ghetto and found out that my mother, my older two sisters, their husbands, and their two babies survived.
And they are in this ghetto.
The name was Glebokie.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He came back, and he told us that.
About my brothers, he didn't know their whereabouts.
When he told us that, we decided that we will go back.
And special I said whatever it is, I'm going.
Where my mother is, I'm going.
He was very good.
He put us in a wagon.
He brought us.
He let us off at the border.
And we, by ourselves, came into the ghetto.
I was the reunited with my mother, with my sisters, and two children.
My oldest sister had a little boy, Fola.
And he was four at that time.
And my younger sister-- I mean, the younger, not me, the younger of my two oldest.
She had a little girl who was Mary, Miriam.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You could imagine our reunion.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother became violent.
And she screamed at my sister that we came back.
And she begged her, she really begged her.
And she almost made her do it, to go back again in hiding.
I didn't listen.
I said, whatever you do to me, I'm not going.
But she arranged that my sister, Celia went back with Piotr to Lapuszyno.
And she was there.
From that time on, I understood that she wasn't hiding.
She was in the house, posing as a Polish niece of their family.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In Glebokie ghetto was also so very, very hard.
They used to take actions every other day.
An action of older people, take them.
And they would surround them, bring them to [INAUDIBLE], where the main pits were constantly open.
Because they used to bring many people, and shot them, and just throw in.
They never closed them, even.
Then they had actions of children.
When I came there, food wasn't any.
So they put me-- they assigned me.
Every day, I had to go and eat in somebody else's house.
And somehow, I became-- either the dirt, or the leeches, I don't know.
I had-- I had scabies.
And they were so deep.
They were eating me up.
My hands, I couldn't hold my fingers.
They were like blisters on my fingers, between my fingers.
And I was panicked by terrible, terrible lice.
I had it all over.
And whatever it was done to me, they-- I couldn't get rid of that.
And I apparently looked also, very awkward.
Because I was growing.
I was very tall and very thin, and-- and so hungry, constantly hungry.
But my mother couldn't help me.
And the food what the people gave me was only once a day.
And I remember, since I looked this way, I wasn't able to sit and get normal at the table.
They used to sort of put me alone like a little puppy and give me the portion what they can afford.
And my hunger never subsided.
My mother saw that there will be no end to the suffering.
And she knew that it's impossible.
Since she was born in a nearby city, Postawy.
And she knew that the ghetto is existing there still, there she had a family.
She had three brothers.
So I actually don't know how she arranged it.
Maybe she still had some gold, or money, or whatever.
She found a gentile man and put us in a wagon.
We were covered all by-- with lots of hay.
And she took us all away to the ghetto of Postawy.
When we arrived there-- Who was with you then?
My mother, my two sisters, their husbands, and the two children, and myself.
Yes.
By that time, we also knew that my brother, Zalman and my brother, [INAUDIBLE] survived.
And they went to the woods.
They had-- they acquired-- I also don't know how they did, but they had guns.
And they were in the woods.
They let Piotr know, because they also found out that Celia, my sister, was there.
And through Piotr, we knew that they survived, and they are in the woods.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Many people of survivors got together in the woods.
And they were hiding at that time.
It wasn't yet nothing organized or anything.
But they were there.
And in this way, we also let know Piotr to tell my brothers that we are not anymore in the ghetto of Glebokie, but we went to Postawy.
Because my brothers thought by any kind of-- maybe they could come to the ghetto there, because it was closer.
And they could maybe take us all out to the woods.
And they would arrange that we should be in the woods for all of us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We stayed in the ghetto.
And my uncles gave us-- by the way, the-- in the ghetto, the houses of my uncles' were in the ghetto.
So we had space where we can sleep.
And they had, at that time, still quite a bit of food.
So we weren't hungry anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We stayed there for several months, until late fall.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they decided to liquidate this ghetto, too.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] This time, they were more thorough.
They had machine guns.
They were really organized.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We start, again, running.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't let my mother out of my hand.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS] It was very, very cold.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we start running.
And the people start falling, falling, falling.
It was-- they were shooting with machine guns.
It was without stopping.
It was back and forth, like.
And they were-- people were falling so much.
And I was running with my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She got shot.
She fell.
[CRYING] [SOBBING] And I stood over her.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SOBBING] She start begging me [CRYING] and promise me that I would run.
And I would run to Lapuszyno, to Celia, that I must promise her.
But those were her last words.
She just died.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I-- I-- I was kneeled down near her.
And I wanted really to keep the promise.
When I got up, I wanted to start running, and I was shot.
I was shot in my leg.
And I saw it was torn out, and the blood would start running.
And also, it was scratched, my head.
And my head-- and my head start bleeding.
And my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my hair.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I was-- I ran for a little bit.
But I felt I couldn't.
I returned to my mother.
And I just lay on the top of her.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The days were short, because it was late in fall.
It was very cold, almost freezing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When the shooting subsided, and they did a marvelous job.
Because from this ghetto, you wouldn't believe it.
Only I and one more person survived.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS] What they did, they went-- they came with lots of wagons and sleds.
And they collected the dead people.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They collected, they threw us in.
I-- intended-- I played dead.
And they would-- two people would just throw the dead people in the wagons and some other means.
They brought us to big pit, which it was very large.
It was open.
And they start throwing the bodies in the pit.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was quite-- it took several hours until they cleaned, and digged, and brought everybody dead.
And they brought us in to the pit.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I was thrown in there, I felt I was alive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I also felt some other people were moving.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING] But I lay there.
When everything stopped, it was very dark.
And everybody left.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They didn't close the pit.
Because, apparently, the thought that they would still collect people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I decided I will try to crawl out of there.
And I first crawled-- I was in the middle.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I first had to crawl to the- to one of the sides.
And from there, I don't know.
Apparently, my will was so strong that I managed to crawl out of there.
But I was so soaked in blood.
And my shoes came off.
I was barefoot.
My hair were all soaked in blood, my clothes.
And I got out of there, and I start running.
I didn't know where, but from far away, I saw light.
And, apparently, it must've been a small farm.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I ran to this house.
I knocked at the door, and a woman came out.
She saw me.
And, apparently, she must have thought I'm a devil or something, because-- you could imagine.
And it was so cold that my hair became like icicles, like red icicles.
And she saw me.
And I asked her if she could give me something to put on my feet.
And she gave me-- not shoes.
She gave me, like not bandages-- like, like [NON-ENGLISH], like old rags and some rope.
And I bound-- I-- I put it on my feet.
And I told the rope and closed it up.
And she gave me some bread.
And I asked her if she could point-- if she would know which way Lapuszyno is.
And she did.
She just pointed which direction.
And I start going this direction.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I tried as much as possible to always be in the ravines or near the wood.
I wouldn't be spotted.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I walked-- it was a whole night.
And I still walked, and walked.
And I walked.
And then it was day, and I still walked.
And it was, apparently, in the late, late afternoon that I suddenly arrived to that river that I recognized.
And looking over that river, I recognized the house of the Bielewicz house.
And I knew that I'm very close.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Before that, I was so excited that I went on to main road.
On the main road was a man in a wagon.
And he caught me.
And it was so close.
And he caught me.
And he put me in-- in the wagon.
He was really drunk.
And he thought he really find the biggest find he could Because anybody who could produce a Jew would get paid, and especially paid with salt.
Salt was a very, very expensive-- and a very-- you couldn't get it.
And he thought that he's going to get quite a bit, because he asked me my name.
I thought, if I will tell him my name, because of my parents that he would maybe let me go.
Because my parents were friends with many, many gentiles.
And my father was a merchant.
And he had to do business with many of them.
I thought, if I'm going to say that my name is Cymer, my father Shmuel.
But, apparently, this made him even happier.
Because in the ghetto, everybody knew who our family was.
So they thought-- he thought that he will do even better business.
And, of course, he didn't let me-- and he--
